description: An intuition may arrive before a person can give the steps behind it. 'thuni' describes a thought, judgement, or response that comes without a consciously followed chain of reasoning, perhaps from long practice, bodily recognition, or an immediate impression. It may still be accurate or mistaken; 'hiru' is the verb intuit, while 'seloi' (insightful) concerns the depth of what was perceived.
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip lightly against the upper teeth and pass unvoiced air for 'th', then raise the back of the tongue for rounded high-back 'u'. Touch the tongue tip to the upper teeth for voiced dental 'n' and finish with unrounded high-front 'i'. Stress falls on 'thu'.
sound_symbolism: Breath passes the teeth into the dark rounding of 'u', then the brief dental nasal turns towards clear 'i'. The short contour may recall an impression arriving whole before a longer account has been spoken.
usage_notes: The quality-noun rule supplies intuition. Calling a judgement intuitive reports its route into awareness; it does not establish truth, evidence, expertise, or a right to act on another person.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Repeated work with weather, soil, materials, or tools can produce recognition before a practitioner can explain every cue. 'thuni' names that route without making experience infallible.

semantic_domains.cognition: a thought or response arising without consciously followed reasoning
semantic_domains.wisdom: immediate judgement that may draw on experience and still remains open to checking
